title: Fault-Tolerant, Rigidity-Preserving Control of Inflatable Truss Robots
html_content:
Fault-Tolerant, Rigidity-Preserving Control of Inflatable Truss Robots
James Wade
вҲ—1
, Isaac Weaver
вҲ—1
, Mihai Stanciu
1
, Nathan Usevitch
1
вҲ—
These authors contributed equally to this work
1
All authors are with the Ira A. Fulton School of Engineering, Mechanical Engineering Department, Brigham Young University, Provo, Utah, US
This work was supported in part by the National Science Foundation under Grant 2501928 and in part by the Utah NASA Space Grant Consortium.
Abstract
Isoperimetric robotic trusses can adapt to different tasks and environments because they have a high strength-to-weight ratio, can change their own shape dramatically, and can be reconfigured into a variety of different shapes. However, motor failures in operational environments can severely limit operational capabilities if not properly addressed. This paper presents a fault-tolerant control framework for an inflatable robotic truss that maintains functionality despite motor failures, shown through three key contributions. First, we extend the kinematic optimization to handle arbitrary combinations of motor failures by imposing equality constraints to ensure failed actuators are not used. Second, we introduce discrete-time control barrier function (DTCBF) constraints that mathematically guarantee structural rigidity while maximizing workspace utilization, a critical requirement for reliable operation of truss robots under discrete-time control. Third, we implement closed-loop position control using onboard encoder feedback and a forward kinematics-based state estimator, improving positional accuracy in the presence of disturbances. We validate our approach through simulation and hardware experiments on a 2D isoperimetric truss testbed. For a 2D configuration with 6 actuators, we demonstrate
>
рқҹ”рқҹ—
%
\mathbf{>69\%}
workspace preservation under single-motor failures and a
>
рқҹҗрқҹ“
%
\mathbf{>25\%}
improvement in tracking accuracy with closed-loop control. These results establish a foundation for more robust and resilient isoperimetric truss robots operating under degraded actuation.
I
Introduction
Reconfigurable truss robots provide a lightweight, deployable, and adaptable framework for robotic structures. Their high stowed-to-deployed volume ratio and ability to morph between configurations make them attractive for applications including remote infrastructure deployment, field robotics, and space systems
[
20
]
. This work focuses on isoperimetric truss robots, which consist of compliant beams routed through motorized rollers. By driving along the tubing, the rollers redistribute tube length, changing the structure geometry under a constant overall perimeter constraint. However, existing control architectures provide limited robustness to actuator failures despite the inherent redundancy of these systems. In tightly coupled truss robots, failure of a single actuator can substantially degrade workspace, manipulability, and tracking performance in a way that has not been characterized. This work develops a fault-tolerant control framework for inflatable isoperimetric truss robots, emphasizing maintenance of rigidity and task-space performance under degraded actuation. Fig.
1
illustrates the hardware platform and control architecture.
Figure 1
:
The 2D isoperimetric truss robot testbed illustrating the closed-loop and failure-aware control architecture. The robot traces a square path (yellow) with its controlled vertex, with the translucent overlay showing a prior configuration. A lightning bolt marks a motor failure, which is communicated to the offboard computer, along with encoder feedback. The onboard controller updates the kinematic model to reflect the failure, estimates the current configuration via forward kinematics (FK), and issues updated motor commands that compensate for the failure via inverse kinematics (IK).
II
System Overview
II-A
Isoperimetric Robots
Isoperimetric robots were first introduced in
[
22
]
, with later work extending them as modular truss-like structures for lunar environments
[
20
]
. Each triangular module consists of an inflated tube shared between two active roller units (Fig.
2
) and one passive roller, forming a triangular face of the truss. Each active roller contains a DC motor that transfers tubing between triangle edges, enabling coordinated shape morphing while the perimeter of each triangle remains constant. The passive roller shares the same mechanical structure but omits the motor and PCB, serving as an anchoring point for the tube ends. This inflatable truss architecture offers a promising alternative to metallic structural robots, combining lightweight transportability with adaptability, strength, and actuation redundancy in several 3D configurations
[
20
]
. However, the tightly coupled actuation means that individual actuator failures can significantly degrade mobility, motivating fault-sensitivity analysis.
II-B
Limitations of Current Controller
A key capability of the robot is moving a user-specified target vertex (Fig.
1
) along a desired trajectory. The current kinematics-based controller solves a quadratic optimization problem to determine the motion of each active roller motor, assuming all actuators are fully functional. In practice, motor failures and imperfections can significantly reduce the performance if the controller cannot adapt to the degraded system state. Therefore, maintaining performance under actuator failures requires an adaptive optimization-based controller.
Figure 2
:
Diagram labeling each part of the truss robotвҖҷs active roller unit. Three roller nodes, along with an inflated tube, form a triangular module of the truss robot.
II-C
Proposed Solution and Contributions
To address these limitations, we developed a robust controller for the isoperimetric robot capable of operating under arbitrary combinations of motor failures. The controller maximizes trajectory accuracy and usable workspace despite reduced actuation capability, while control barrier functions enable safe navigation along workspace boundaries. The resulting system is more resilient to actuator failures and better suited for reliability-critical operations. This work makes the following contributions:
вҖў
Analysis of robustness of the motion of an isoperimetric robot under motor failures, including workspace and manipulability degradation.
вҖў
Discrete-time control barrier function formulation that guarantees structural rigidity while maximizing workspace utilization in sampled-data systems.
вҖў
Closed-loop position control using onboard sensing and forward kinematics-based state estimation for an inflatable isoperimetric robot.
III
Related Work
Truss-based Robotic Systems
Truss-based robotic and structural systems have been widely explored for their high strength-to-weight ratio and efficient load-bearing capabilities. NASA has researched the use of trusses made from lunar regolith
[
13
]
and trusses composed of deployable beams
[
16
]
for extraterrestrial operation. There are a variety of different actuated truss structures in research literature, including variable geometry trusses (VGT)
[
11
,
19
]
, tensegrity-based robots
[
14
]
, and tetrahedral 3D-printed robots
[
15
]
. While these systems provide mechanical redundancy, they typically operate under fundamentally different kinematic constraints from the constant-perimeter requirements of our isoperimetric system, necessitating a distinct approach to fault-tolerant control. Our truss robot also differs from these structures in that it is both 1) a compliant structure that 2) is robotic and can dynamically change shape, making it more versatile to both typical and novel use cases. Our robotвҖҷs method of actuation differs from most existing truss actuation approaches, using its internal compliant tubes to morph its shape rather than linear actuators or tensioned strings.
Control of Redundant and Coupled Robotic Systems
Redundancy has long been a central topic in the field of robotics
[
12
]
, focusing on the resolution of kinematic redundancy to satisfy secondary objectives such as singularity avoidance or fault tolerance
[
18
,
9
]
. Prior work has extensively examined the importance of maintaining graph rigidity (which is critical for preserving structural shape in truss-like systems) in both theory and practice, and much of this research has often been situated in the context of swarm robotics
[
8
,
3
]
. Some of this research has even explored motion planning for load manipulation tasks using swarms
[
10
]
, similar to the load-bearing capabilities of our truss robot
[
20
]
. However, our approach differs fundamentally. In contrast to swarmsвҖ”where individual agents move semi-independentlyвҖ”our truss robot exhibits tightly coupled motion, where displacement of a single vertex induces correlated motion across the entire structure. Such behavior is more analogous to hyper-redundant and continuum architectures
[
5
,
6
]
, which are characterized by a high degree of kinematic coupling that requires whole-body coordination. As in continuum and hyper-redundant systems, feasible motion in robotic truss structures emerges from coordinated whole-body deformation rather than independent agent motion. This intrinsic interdependence means that graph-based swarm methods do not directly transfer to our system, motivating extensions to the kinematic and control framework tailored to tightly coupled truss structures. In our framework, this redundancy is leveraged not only for workspace expansion, but also to satisfy rigidity-preserving safety constraints through coordinated whole-body motion.
Control Barrier Functions
The operational necessity of workspace utilization must be balanced equally with guarantees of rigidity preservation. Control barrier functions (CBFs) have emerged as a powerful tool for safety-critical control
[
2
]
, where the control input is minimally modified to maintain a nonnegative function
h
вҖӢ
(
x
)
h(x)
that defines a safe region. For sampled-data systems such as ours, discrete-time control barrier functions (DTCBFs) provide rigorous safety guarantees between sampling instances
[
1
,
27
]
. Recent work has established sufficient conditions for forward invariance in discrete-time systems
[
4
]
, which we leverage to ensure structural rigidity throughout operation. However, DTCBFs have not previously been applied to rigidity maintenance in robotic truss systems, where redundant actuation can be leveraged to maximize workspace utilization while satisfying rigidity-preserving safety constraints.
IV
Methods
This section reviews the kinematic framework from
[
22
]
and presents our novel contributions: fault-tolerant actuation, rigidity preservation, and closed-loop position control. Fig.
3
shows the kinematic model of the 2D truss robot, as well as the hardware setup.
Figure 3
:
Two-dimensional truss robot testbed. (a) Kinematic model showing the simplified truss structure (translucent triangles) and the offset model accounting for roller geometry (blue triangles), with active rollers R1вҖ“R6 labeled at each edge. (b) Physical hardware testbed with vertices V1вҖ“V6 and rollers R1вҖ“R6 annotated; yellow arrows indicate positive roller motion direction.

Fig.

The simplified model treats each roller as coincident with its vertex, while the offset model accounts for the physical displacement between the roller contact point and the joint center. The offset model is used for controller state estimation, while the simplified model is used for kinematic analysis since it generalizes to other truss robots beyond the isoperimetric case. IV-C

A failed roller module can be detected when it does not respond to commanded motions within a specified time interval, measurable via encoder feedback. IV-D
Rigidity Preservation via Discrete-Time Barrier Functions

IV-F
Closed-Loop Control with Forward Kinematics Estimator
Given knowledge of the current configuration of the robot, the augmented optimization problem solves for the optimal vertex velocities to produce a desired movement of the target vertex. We build upon this open-loop control to incorporate closed-loop feedback using onboard motor encoders. The optimizer is solved at each control timestep using the latest state estimate, forming a closed-loop control architecture.

This paper extends the control process by reading and transmitting the motor encoder information from each roller module to the central computer. These encoder values are used to estimate the average roller velocities

The vertex positions are updated via forward Euler integration with fixed substepping. Since
J

This iterative refinement improves state estimation accuracy when the Jacobian varies significantly over a single timestep, which is expected given the robotвҖҷs low control frequency. Although higher-order integration methods such as RK4 could further improve accuracy, the small substep size sufficiently limits accumulated integration error when using forward Euler integration.
IV-G
Experimental Platform and Validation Setup
To visualize the results of this research, we constructed a two-dimensional isoperimetric robot comprised of three triangles. Any combination of minimal rigid-body constraints can be used, and we chose to fix one corner (bottom-left of Fig.
3
) in both planar directions and another corner (bottom-right of Fig.
3
) in only the y-direction. This constraint configuration removes the planar rigid-body modes (two translations and one rotation) while avoiding over-constraint of the structure. This setup allows for a clearer visualization of the controllerвҖҷs performance in hardware, as well as workspace and manipulability visualizations in simulation. Due to the communication latency of the radio modules, the controller operates at 2вҖүHz; the substepping scheme described in Section
IV-F
compensates for the resulting large inter-sample configuration changes.
We validate our control algorithms in simulation and hardware experiments. A Python-based simulation environment was developed to test 2D truss configurations. Hardware validation is performed on the 2D testbed shown in Fig.
3
.
V
Results
V-A
Modeling Degraded Kinematics Due to Roller Failure
To simulate roller failures, we apply the constraint specified in Eq.
6
, which fixes a rollerвҖҷs position along its tube. Fig.
5
(left) demonstrates this capability by comparing the nominal case, where all rollers are functional as the robot traces a square path, against a degraded scenario where Roller 1 is constrained to zero motion. Fig.
5
(right) shows the effect of this optimization on the configuration of the robot while still tracing the same path. This constraint framework can be extended to any number of rollers, enabling simulation of arbitrary combinations of roller failures.
Figure 5
:
Effect of constraining Roller 1 to zero displacement via (
6
). (Left) Roller positions over time for the nominal all rollers on [solid] and Roller #1 broken [dashed] cases; In the broken case, Roller 1 [blue] is fixed at zero while other rollers compensate. (Right) Overlay of nominal and broken configurations at the same target vertex position, showing differing internal geometry. Despite the internal reconfiguration, the robot traces an identical square trajectory.
Beyond tracking roller positions, we can quantify the kinematic impact of failures through manipulability analysis at the robotвҖҷs tip. Using the Jacobian from Eq.
11
, which maps roller velocities to Cartesian velocities, we compute the manipulability metric as
M
=
det
вҖӢ
(
J
вҖӢ
J
вҠӨ
)
M=\sqrt{\text{det}(JJ^{\top})}
(12)
[
25
]
. In 2D, this metric is proportional to the area of the manipulability ellipse shown in Fig.
6
. To quantify the spread of this ellipse, we use the condition number of
J
J
, which is the ratio of the major-axis length to the minor-axis length, measuring the robotвҖҷs isotropy at the target vertex
[
17
]
.
Figure 6
:
Manipulability analysis of the target vertex as the 2D robot traces a square path. The bottom-left diagram shows the location of each roller module within the 2D truss. The center plot shows the manipulability retention percentage when each individual roller fails, normalized by the maximum manipulability achieved during this trajectory. The right snapshots visualize the corresponding manipulability ellipses at four time points, with the cyan ellipse representing full manipulability and colored ellipses showing degraded manipulability for each failed roller.
To analyze failure scenarios, we exploit the structure of the Jacobian (Eq.
11
): each column represents the contribution of an individual roller to the robotвҖҷs task-space motion. By zeroing the
i
i
th column, we obtain a degraded Jacobian that models the instantaneous effect of the
i
i
th roller failing at that configuration. Computing this degraded manipulability metric for each roller at every time step reveals how critical each roller is to the robotвҖҷs motion capability throughout the trajectory. Fig.
6
presents this analysis, showing both the time evolution of manipulability when individual rollers fail (normalized by the maximum value during this trajectory), and visual snapshots of the corresponding manipulability ellipses at key time points. The overlaid ellipses provide an intuitive geometric representation of how each roller failure degrades the robotвҖҷs directional motion capabilities. The manipulability index grows as the robot extends outward, reflecting the kinematic structureвҖҷs combination of revolute- and prismatic-type motion. However, a large manipulability index alone can be misleading вҖ” the condition number of the Jacobian is therefore also reported to characterize the ellipseвҖҷs isotropy. As expected, the condition number worsens as the robot extends further from its neutral configuration.
V-B
Degradation of Workspace due to Node Failure
Figure 7
:
Workspace analysis of the truss robot, showing the effective workspace of the robot with individual or a combination of roller units deactivated. (Left) The effect of individual roller unit failure on the robotвҖҷs workspace. The greedy optimal order of failure is 4,3,1,5,6,2. (Right) The effect of combined roller unit failure on the robotвҖҷs workspace, following the greedy optimal order from Fig.
7
(Left).
Fig.
7
shows the effect of roller failures on the robotвҖҷs workspace, computed by commanding the target vertex to extend radially as far as possible in uniformly distributed directions until the optimizer finds no valid solution
[
23
]
. As shown in Fig.
7
, individual roller failures produce asymmetric workspace reductions, with some rollers having a substantially greater impact than others. Single roller failures preserve at least 69% of the nominal workspace area, demonstrating the robotвҖҷs inherent robustness to individual actuator losses. To show the combined effect of failed roller modules, Fig.
7
follows the greedy optimal order (4, 3, 1, 5, 6, 2), showing that even under sequential failures, the workspace remains feasible for many applications.
V-C
Rigidity Maintenance with DTCBF Constraints
Fig.
8
compares the robotвҖҷs workspace with and without the DTCBF formulation; without it, the robot halts as soon as
Пғ

\sigma_{\text{crit}}
reaches the minimum threshold. The DTCBF-enabled workspace achieves a 69% increase in reachable area, confirming that the barrier function actively exploits null-space motion rather than enforcing a hard rigidity cutoff. Fig.
8
also confirms that
Пғ

\sigma_{\text{crit}}
remains within the safe region defined by
h

throughout, as shown with the example trajectory color-coded by time. Therefore, this DTCBF not only maintains rigidity, as did previous methods
[

]
, but also enlarges the workspace by reconfiguring the robot within the null space of the task-space Jacobian.

V-D
Effect of Closed Loop Control on Positional Accuracy
Fig.
9
compares open-loop (OL) and closed-loop (CL) performance across various operational scenarios, where the target vertex traces a square path by visiting each corner sequentially. When a roller failure is present, we further distinguish between open-loop control that is unaware of the failure and open-loop control that explicitly accounts for it. Table
I
reports the root mean square error (RMSE) of each trajectory relative to the nominal, along with the performance improvements over open-loop control. The optimization solves in
27
27
ms (median, mean
33
33
ms; 99th percentile
120
120
ms), comfortably within the
500
500
ms control loop. The solverвҖҷs 100-iteration cap was reached in rare cases, producing worst-case solve times of
вҲј
750
{\sim}750
ms.
The improvements in Table
I
stem from two sources: positional feedback from the encoder-based state estimator, and explicit awareness of failed roller modules within the optimizer. Table
I
demonstrates that closed-loop control consistently outperforms open-loop control, improving trajectory accuracy by at least 25% across all conditions. Closed-loop control improves positional tracking accuracy by correcting for imperfect onboard microcontroller velocity control. To illustrate this, Fig.
9
(right) shows the open-loop and closed-loop comparison when Roller 6вҖҷs DC motor is 50% slower than the kinematic model expects. Open-loop tracking suffers, whereas closed-loop tracking still reaches the same corner positions as in the fully-functional scenario.
Failure-aware open-loop control also yields meaningful gains over its unaware counterpart, confirming that explicitly accounting for roller failures in the kinematic model compensates for lost actuation. The OL-aware RMSE values in Table
I
for the broken scenarios remain close to the fully-functional OL baseline, indicating that the constraints in Eq.
10
enable the controller to redistribute actuation and maintain tracking performance despite the reduced actuator set.

VI
Discussion
These analyses collectively inform users of this robotвҖҷs capabilities and operational considerations. The robotвҖҷs triangular modules allow assembly in many configurations for the same task; however, factors such as the risk of roller failure should guide users in placing more reliable hardware elements at critical locations. Through normal operation, differences in hardware condition may emerge over time, causing some rollers to become more reliable than others. Forecasting which motor failure would most impact task performance is essential when assembling and deploying the robot. Due to the generality of the manipulability index, manipulability ellipses, and the condition number, similar manipulability results as Fig.
6
can be produced for any configuration of the robot. Likewise, the workspace analysis (Fig.
7
) can be extended for any isoperimetric truss robot. Beyond which rollers fail, the workspace and manipulability are also sensitive to
where
in the trajectory they fail; the same tools used here can be applied to analyze degraded configurations at any point during operation. Additionally, these tools can identify which active rollers contribute minimally to a given task and could therefore be replaced with passive rollers without sacrificing performance, directly informing cost-effective robot design for specific applications.
The trajectories in Fig.
9
show imperfect motion and increased error in the Y direction across all scenarios. The closed-loop controller successfully widens the trajectory to better match the desired trajectory, but the height of the produced trajectory appears largely unaffected by closed-loop vs open-loop control. As such, we believe that the source of this error is the bowing in the inflated tubes. An example of this bowing is observed in the upper triangle of Fig.
1
. This bowing has accumulated over time due to repeated use and
prolonged loading of the inflated tubes. Since the closed-loop controller receives positional feedback via encoders, it does not model the compliance in the tubes beyond what is captured by the idealized rigidity model in the DTCBF. Future work will include characterizing this deformation and accounting for it within the closed-loop controller.
Further improvements to the closed-loop controller are possible. The trajectory curvature shown in Fig.
9
(right) under imperfect velocity control could be reduced by optimizing not only distance to the goal position, but also deviation from the desired straight-line path. This and further trajectory planning improvements are left for future work.
VII
Conclusion
Continued advancements in truss robot design could pave the way for soft, inflatable, and adaptable alternatives to rigid, heavy, and metallic structures. This work has enhanced the robustness of isoperimetric robots in three ways. Firstly, it has introduced a controller that allows the robot to operate without using a subset of its active roller modules. We measured the effect of removing different rollers in terms of both manipulability and workspace reduction. Secondly, we implemented a DTCBF to allow the robot to avoid singular configurations. Thirdly, we implemented closed-loop control, allowing the robot to be robust to conditions where the individual rollers fail to move at the commanded speed. These improvements contribute to the development of more robust, lightweight, and reconfigurable robotic truss systems for operation in challenging environments.

preview_text:
Isoperimetric robotic trusses can adapt to different tasks and environments because they have a high strength-to-weight ratio, can change their own shape dramatically, and can be reconfigured into a variety of different shapes. However, motor failures in operational environments can severely limit operational capabilities if not properly addressed. This paper presents a fault-tolerant control framework for an inflatable robotic truss that maintains functionality despite motor failures, shown through three key contributions. First, we extend the kinematic optimization to handle arbitrary combinations of motor failures by imposing equality constraints to ensure failed actuators are not used. Second, we introduce discrete-time control barrier function (DTCBF) constraints that mathematically guarantee structural rigidity while maximizing workspace utilization, a critical requirement for reliable operation of truss robots under discrete-time control. Third, we implement closed-loop position control using onboard encoder feedback and a forward kinematics-based state estimator, improving positional accuracy in the presence of disturbances. We validate our approach through simulation and hardware experiments on a 2D isoperimetric truss testbed. For a 2D configuration with 6 actuators, we demonstrate >69% workspace preservation under single-motor failures and a >25% improvement in tracking accuracy with closed-loop control. These results establish a foundation for more robust and resilient isoperimetric truss robots operating under degraded actuation.

Fault-Tolerant, Rigidity-Preserving Control of Inflatable Truss Robots
James Wade
вҲ—1
, Isaac Weaver
вҲ—1
, Mihai Stanciu
1
, Nathan Usevitch
1
вҲ—
These authors contributed equally to this work
1
All authors are with the Ira A. Fulton School of Engineering, Mechanical Engineering Department, Brigham Young University, Provo, Utah, US
This work was supported in part by the National Science Foundation under Grant 2501928 and in part
